skill.name: azure-mgmt-apimanagement-py
skill.description: Reference guide for using the Azure API Management Python SDK to manage APIM services, APIs, products, subscriptions, policies, named values, backends, and users.
skill.summary: Manage Azure API Management resources with Python SDK examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported code execution, network, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but the reviewed evidence is mostly Markdown code examples for Azure SDK usage. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found, but the guide includes examples that print a subscription key and place a placeholder secret directly in code, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Subscription Key Printed in Example
medium_findings.description: The subscription example prints subscription.primary_key. If copied into real automation, this can expose an APIM subscription key through logs, terminals, or AI transcripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly formats and prints the primary key value. The skill is documentation, not executable malware, but the security risk in the recommended example is clear.

medium_findings.title: Secret Value Shown Inline in Code Example
medium_findings.description: The named value example places a placeholder secret directly in the source snippet. This encourages hardcoded secret handling instead of reading from a secure source such as Azure Key Vault or an environment-specific secret store.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly creates a secret named value with an inline value. It appears instructional rather than malicious, but the unsafe pattern is concrete.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The dynamic import and external command detections occur inside fenced Markdown snippets that demonstrate package installation and Azure SDK calls. No evidence found that the skill itself executes these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is Markdown documentation with code fences. There is no script file or automation wrapper that would run the snippets without user action.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs Are Demonstration Inputs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are example OpenAPI and backend URLs used to demonstrate APIM import and backend configuration. No evidence found of unauthorized network calls or credential exfiltration by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs appear as parameter values inside Azure SDK examples. They are not contacted by the skill unless a user intentionally adapts and runs the sample.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is Limited to Subscription ID
low_findings.description: The environment access example reads AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID for client initialization. This is normal Azure SDK usage and does not show secret harvesting or broad environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only environment variable shown is a specific Azure subscription identifier. There is no loop over environment variables or network transmission of environment data.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The reviewed lines flagged for weak cryptography contain skill metadata, OpenAPI sample content, a product description, and backend description text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines does not show cryptographic code or algorithm names. These appear to be false positives from generic pattern matching.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Exposure Through Output
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide demonstrates printing a subscription primary key. Avoid displaying keys in logs or terminal output when adapting the example.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippet explicitly prints a key property. The risk depends on user execution, but the dangerous output pattern is direct.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Secret Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide demonstrates setting a secret named value from an inline string. Use secure secret retrieval instead of embedding real secret values in source files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The value is a placeholder, but the example structure is a known unsafe secret-management pattern when copied into production code.

content.user_title: Manage Azure API Management with Python
content.value_statement: Azure API Management setup often requires many SDK calls across services, APIs, products, policies, and users. This skill provides focused Python examples for common APIM management tasks.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install azure-mgmt-apimanagement and azure-identity packages.
Provides examples for creating APIM services, APIs, products, subscriptions, and users.
Shows API import from inline OpenAPI content or an OpenAPI URL.
Includes examples for API policies, named values, backend services, and operation groups.
limitations: It is a reference guide, not a runnable automation package.
Examples use placeholder names and must be adapted to real Azure resource groups.
It does not validate Azure permissions, quotas, regions, or live service availability.
Some snippets need safer secret handling before production use.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap APIM Resources
use_cases.description: Create an APIM service and import a first API using Python SDK patterns.

use_cases.title: Configure API Access
use_cases.description: Create products, associate APIs, and manage subscriptions for API consumers.

use_cases.title: Apply Gateway Settings
use_cases.description: Add policies, named values, backends, and users while reviewing APIM operation groups.

prompt_templates.title: Install and Authenticate
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to install the Azure API Management Python SDK and create an authenticated ApiManagementClient.

prompt_templates.title: Create an APIM Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me adapt the APIM service creation example for my resource group, service name, region, publisher email, and SKU.

prompt_templates.title: Import and Publish an API
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline the Python SDK steps for importing an OpenAPI definition, creating a product, and adding the API to that product.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production APIM Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure API Management Python script for unsafe key output, hardcoded secrets, policy mistakes, and missing error handling.

output_examples.input: Create a new APIM service with the Python SDK.
output_examples.output: A concise sequence covering client creation, service parameters, Developer SKU selection, and the create-or-update operation.

output_examples.input: Import an OpenAPI API and attach it to a product.
Add a rate limit policy to an API.
output_examples.output: A policy-oriented explanation that identifies the API scope, policy XML structure, and create-or-update call.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential with least-privilege Azure roles for APIM management.
Keep real secrets in Key Vault or approved secret stores, not in source snippets.
Review generated policies and API definitions before applying them to production gateways.
anti_patterns: Printing subscription keys or other credentials during automation runs.
Copying placeholder resource names into production scripts without validation.
Using broad Azure permissions when a narrower APIM management role is sufficient.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides Python SDK examples that a user must adapt and run separately.

faq.question: Which package does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on azure-mgmt-apimanagement with azure-identity for authentication.

faq.question: Can it create APIs from OpenAPI definitions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for inline OpenAPI JSON and OpenAPI documents referenced by URL.

faq.question: Does it cover APIM policies?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an API policy example with rate limiting and header configuration.

faq.question: Is it production ready as written?
faq.answer: No. The snippets need real resource names, error handling, permission checks, and secure secret handling.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
